course_title: Masterclass- Advanced Research in the Humanities

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an understanding of the basic principles of interdisciplinary research.

Be able to design relevant and feasible approaches to investigating research problems, including undertaking research at honours level, creating your own research questions, methodological approaches, and research designs.
Demonstrate knowledge of practical and theoretical issues underpinning work with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people and communities.
Understand the types of discourses in which work with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people is situated - both historically and today - and your own positioning to these.
Use library and electronic resources at a level appropriate for your research and be able to locate appropriate materials for your area of study.
Communicate and interact competently and professionally in an industry environment (using a range of communication methods).
Demonstrate your skills and strengths in various career development situations.

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Project, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Participation is essential for passing this course, and requires you to interact respectfully and learn from your peers. Students are expected to participate in tutorial discussions and complete tutorial tasks. This assessment requires you to show evidence of preparation for seminars and participate in large and small group discussions with relevant and well-focused comments. Participation in non-compulsory activities (like lectures and events organised through other students' internships) is not essential, but will earn you additional points. 


The participation component in this this course is slightly larger than other humanities courses, as it includes weekly pass/fail research or storying exercises in weeks 2-4 and 10-13, which should be completed before tutorials.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Tutorial exercises will be submitted through an assessment portal on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submissions will be accepted until the end of the semester.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description: You should introduce 2-3 draft research questions in a BRIEF document (350 words in length, excluding bibliography), presenting a GENERAL area of study, a potential research approach, and an indicative bibliography (ca 3-5 sources) within the context of your preferred major. This is a research exercise, evaluating your skills, knowledge and approach in acquiring relevant research material - and it does not need to be related to your future honours plans (if you are planning to undertake honours). You will receive feedback on the level of sophistication of the formulation of your research questions in relation to your topic, sources and approach.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: This five-week mini-internship, running from week 5 - week 9, gives you a unique opportunity to get to know your university from a new perspective. In week 3, you will be assigned an internship role in one of our 20+ internships with various organisational units and teams throughout the university. You will be required to complete a suite of tasks (set by your internship supervisor), and submit a final portfolio, along with a 500-word internship report, outlining what you have achieved, and any challenges you may have had to overcome. Throughout your internship you will also be required to fill out an internship diary, to track your progress. The quality of your work will be assessed by your internship supervisor and the course coordinator.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit Internship Report and Portfolio through Blackboard
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
16/10/2026 2:00 pm

A schedule for the oral component will be released on 6/10/26 - students will sign up for a preferred 15-minute time slot.
assessments.task_description:
You will be given a 15-minute time slot within a set of of exam times in weeks 12 and 13 to complete a mock-job interview in front of a panel of examiners, drawing on your recent mini-internship experience, your understanding of work and research ethics working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people acquired in the course, and your experience of the Advanced Humanities program as a whole.

You must also submit your current CV , a 'Cover Letter' (ca 500 words) and a 'Response to Selection Criteria' (ca 1,500 words), based on the information mentioned above, and a chosen 'position description' provided on Blackboard via Turnitin by Friday 26/09/25 at 2pm.

The 'position description', 'selection criteria' and interview time slots will be released two weeks in advance, on Friday 12/09/25 at 2pm.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic: MID-SEMESTER BREAK
learning_activities.topic_description: MID-SEMESTER BREAK

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1 Lecture: INTRODUCTION TO THE COURSE: The Future of Work, Humanities Research, and Cultural Collaboration

LECTURE: Introduction to interdisciplinary research, the differences and similarities between humanities disciplines, and the future work place.

PRE-RECORDED INTERVIEWS: Mixed UQ HASS Staff

THERE ARE NO TUTORIALS THIS WEEK


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L07
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 1 Lecture: INTRODUCTION TO THE COURSE: The Future of Work, Humanities Research, and Cultural Collaboration
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1 Lecture: INTRODUCTION TO THE COURSE: The Future of Work, Humanities Research, and Cultural Collaboration LECTURE: Introduction to interdisciplinary research, the differences and similarities between humanities disciplines, and the future work place. PRE-RECORDED INTERVIEWS: Mixed UQ HASS Staff THERE ARE NO TUTORIALS THIS WEEK

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 Lecture: RESEARCH TRAJECTORIES: Building Respect and Connections

ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The first half of your lecture this week will be co-taught with the ATSIS unit, and you will hear Associate Professor Katelyn Barney speak about how you build a successful relationship with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people and communities.

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: The second half of the lecture will focus on how you may start to formulate a humanities research project, emphasising the importance of honing in and refining a topic. We will also discuss the mini-internships that form part of this course.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 2 Lecture: RESEARCH TRAJECTORIES: Building Respect and Connections
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2 Lecture: RESEARCH TRAJECTORIES: Building Respect and Connections ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The first half of your lecture this week will be co-taught with the ATSIS unit, and you will hear Associate Professor Katelyn Barney speak about how you build a successful relationship with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people and communities. METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: The second half of the lecture will focus on how you may start to formulate a humanities research project, emphasising the importance of honing in and refining a topic. We will also discuss the mini-internships that form part of this course.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 Tutorial

WORKSHOP: We will discuss different disciplinary methodologies and start working in discipline-specific groups to start workshopping your preferred research areas. We will discuss respectful ways of negotiating research relationships, including how to contact potential community mentors and supervisors, and how to negotiate a project with them. We will also workshop ways of formulating appropriate email conversations with people from different communities, and you will be encouraged to consider your choice of mini-internship for the course.

PRACTICAL TASK: Supervisor/Mentor Email

REQUIRED READINGS: Eco, Lewis and Bunda & Phillips (through Blackboard)


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3 Lecture: RESEARCH TRAJECTORIES: Formulating the Right Questions

ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The first half of your lecture this week will be co-taught with the ATSIS unit, and you will hear Quandamooka researcher and educator Dr Ren Perkins talk about how we can build more diversity into education in Queensland, and ways of making classrooms more welcoming to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people.

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: In the second half of the lecture, we will discuss how you go about communicating your research ideas clearly and efficiently when you first start formulating a project. We will consider how to spark the interest of a diverse audience by formulating the right research questions.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: In the second half of the lecture, we will discuss how you go about communicating your research ideas clearly and efficiently when you first start formulating a project. learning_activities.topic:
Week 3 Tutorial

WORKSHOP: We will discuss research and work trajectories and consider the role our cultural, economic, religious, gender, and material background plays in the way we learn, and the opportunities available to us. We will also workshop ways of organising your research, and formulating ideas into a set of productive research questions.

PRACTICAL TASK: Your Internship Choices & Rationale

REQUIRED READING: Booth, Eco and Fredericks et. al., through Blackboard.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

PRACTICAL TASK: Your Internship Choices & Rationale REQUIRED READING: Booth, Eco and Fredericks et. al., through Blackboard.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 Lecture: RESEARCH TRAJECTORIES: Rethinking the Purpose of Research

ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The first half of your lecture this week will be co-taught with the ATSIS unit, and you will hear Bidjara woman Professor Anne Pattel-Gray explore ideas of reciprocity, and the importance of developing strong relationships when working with Aboriginal people. She will also consider various aspects of ethics surrounding historical research.

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: In the second half of the lecture, we will discuss how humanities research and the tasks you've been doing throughout your degrees are in fact work skills. We will develop these practical skills to refine a research topic, find your original research contribution, alongside a discussion of how research skills may be used in a variety of future career paths. We will also discuss UQ Research Culture, Practice, and Resources and Ethics.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 Tutorial

WORKSHOP: We will discuss 'ownership of history', ethical considerations in humanities research, practical work and research management skills, and the various ways in which humanities research may be translated into more mainstream humanities work.

PRACTICAL EXERCISE: Career Trajectory Exercise

REQUIRED READING: Watego, Booth, Eco, through Blackboard

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.topic:
SHUT-UP-AND-WRITE SESSION

This optional shut-up-and-write session running over Zoom on Thursday 20/6 at 9am-11am, will be offered as a means of sitting down together and get your first assessment for the course written in one go! It will be an opportunity to try out a traditional 'Pomodoro session'.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5 Lecture: WORK TRAJECTORIES: How to Make the Most Out of your Work

ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The first half of your lecture this week will be co-taught with the ATSIS unit, where Associate Professor Steve Salisbury will discuss how he has built and sustained relationships with Indigenous communities to aid his work in palaeontology (finding dinosaurs!).

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: In the second half of the lecture, we will discuss how to make the most out of your internship period.

Learning outcomes: L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6 Lecture: CULTURAL TRAJECTORIES: Engagement and Project Lifecycles

ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The first half of your lecture this week will be co-taught with the ATSIS unit, where Yimen and Goreng Gorgeng man Jim Walker will discuss key considerations underpinning work with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people and communities. He will consider project lifecycle issues, including, project planning, relationship building and consultation, project implementation, participation, project reporting, dissemination and ongoing relationships.

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: In the second half of the lecture, we will discuss how to build your portfolio of work for submission at the end of your internship period.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8 Lecture: WORK TRAJECTORIES: Education & Academia

In this lecture, you will get an introduction to what a career in education or academia may look like, with representatives discussing primary and secondary education from UQ's School of Education and representatives discussing academic work and tertiary education from UQ's School of Historical and Philosophical Inquiry and UQ's Centre for Western Civilisation. We will discuss the ways in which you could start such a journey - for example, the qualifications you need, and ways in which you can test if it suits you, and extra-curricular activities that may boost your CV.


Invited Guests: TBA (teachers and academics)

Learning outcomes: L06, L07

Week 9 Lecture: WORK TRAJECTORIES: the GLAM Sector

In this lecture, representatives from the UQ Art Museum, the UQ library and the Integrated Pathology Learning Centre will discuss what a career in museum, library and curatorial spaces may look like, and what you may need to do to get there.


Invited Guests: TBA (curators, librarians and people working in government)

Learning outcomes: L06, L07

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10 Lecture: CULTURAL TRAJECTORIES: Working With Indigenous People

PRE-RECORDED METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: All lecture material will be pre-recorded this week, due to the King's birthday public holiday. In the first half of the lecture, we will think through ideas, principles and theories of academic research and writing, specifically by looking in further detail at Tracey Bunda and Louise Phillips's 'storying' approach to research. Alongside Bunda & Phillip's work we will also discuss the key research ethics presented by Jim Walker - how to identify ethical questions and issues in your own research, how to manage these, and how to navigate them. We will consider the ways in which projects developed in collaboration with Indigenous people may take shape, and discuss key considerations underpinning work with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people and communities.

ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The second half of your lecture this week will be a recorded lecture from the ATSIS unit, where Professor Tracey Bunda will introduce you to 'storying' through her own deep ancestral connection to this concept, and the ethical mindset needed to conduct this work.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 10 Lecture: CULTURAL TRAJECTORIES: Working With Indigenous People
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 10 Lecture: CULTURAL TRAJECTORIES: Working With Indigenous People PRE-RECORDED METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: All lecture material will be pre-recorded this week, due to the King's birthday public holiday. In the first half of the lecture, we will think through ideas, principles and theories of academic research and writing, specifically by looking in further detail at Tracey Bunda and Louise Phillips's 'storying' approach to research. Alongside Bunda & Phillip's work we will also discuss the key research ethics presented by Jim Walker - how to identify ethical questions and issues in your own research, how to manage these, and how to navigate them. We will consider the ways in which projects developed in collaboration with Indigenous people may take shape, and discuss key considerations underpinning work with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people and communities. ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The second half of your lecture this week will be a recorded lecture from the ATSIS unit, where Professor Tracey Bunda will introduce you to 'storying' through her own deep ancestral connection to this concept, and the ethical mindset needed to conduct this work.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10 Tutorial

WORKSHOP: In our tutorials we will discuss the difference between working 'with' and 'for' Indigenous people. We will also discuss 'Storying' as a methodology, and we will talk about ways in which we can use 'Storying' in our research and work lives. Alongside this week's lecture material, you'll need to revisit Jim Walker's week 6 lecture for this class.

PRACTICAL EXERCISE: Positioning Exercise

REQUIRED READING: Bunda & Phillips, Janke, ATSIS, through Blackboard

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L07

PRACTICAL EXERCISE: Positioning Exercise REQUIRED READING: Bunda & Phillips, Janke, ATSIS, through Blackboard

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 Lecture: CULTURAL TRAJECTORIES: Engagement & Project Lifecycles

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: In the first half of the lecture, we will discuss how you build a project from an original idea, and how to find and sustain good relationships with collaborators, mentors and supervisors to support your work. It is about the importance of thinking expansively and between disciplines and communities! We will look at Steve Salisbury's work as an example of somebody who builds collaborations that stretch across many different layers of our community.

RECORDED LECTURE SEGMENT: The second half of your lecture this week will be a recorded lecture segment by Professor Anna Johnston, from the School of Communication and Arts, who will tell you about one of her many research projects, and how she went about building this idea, step by step.

RECORDED LECTURE SEGMENT: The second half of your lecture this week will be a recorded lecture segment by Professor Anna Johnston, from the School of Communication and Arts, who will tell you about one of her many research projects, and how she went about building this idea, step by step.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 Tutorial

WORKSHOP: In our tutorials, we will explore ideas of reciprocity, and the importance of developing strong relationships when working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people, and when working collaboratively in general. Alongside the ideas presented in the week 11 lecture, you'll need to revisit Steve Salisbury's week 5 lecture for this class.

PRACTICAL EXERCISE: AI Research Question Exercise

REQUIRED READING: Bunda & Phillips, Eco, Booth, ABC through Blackboard.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

PRACTICAL EXERCISE: AI Research Question Exercise REQUIRED READING: Bunda & Phillips, Eco, Booth, ABC through Blackboard.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 Lecture: CULTURAL TRAJECTORIES: Locating Selves & Ethical Questions

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: In the first half of the lecture, we will consider language and naming processes. Why do names matter? Do we truly lose our identity if we lose our language and is there a way to recover them? If so, how do you go about recovering something that in some cases has been lost for generations? In this class, we'll revisit Samantha Disbray's lecture from week 7, alongside some new considerations from history and creative writing. We will continue Bunda & Phillips's discussions about 'storying', specifically considering the response-abilities it entails. We'll discuss what it means to write history, and taking ownership of a story.

ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The second half of your lecture this week will be a recorded lecture from the ATSIS unit, where the multi-award-winning Aboriginal author Professor Anita Heiss will discuss what goes into writing stories that are about your OWN history, and stories that matter to your people. She'll discuss why history and stories matter, and why they are always-already personal.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 12 Lecture: CULTURAL TRAJECTORIES: Locating Selves & Ethical Questions

Why do names matter? Do we truly lose our identity if we lose our language and is there a way to recover them? If so, how do you go about recovering something that in some cases has been lost for generations? In this class, we'll revisit Samantha Disbray's lecture from week 7, alongside some new considerations from history and creative writing. We will continue Bunda & Phillips's discussions about 'storying', specifically considering the response-abilities it entails. We'll discuss what it means to write history, and taking ownership of a story. ATSIS LECTURE SEGMENT: The second half of your lecture this week will be a recorded lecture from the ATSIS unit, where the multi-award-winning Aboriginal author Professor Anita Heiss will discuss what goes into writing stories that are about your OWN history, and stories that matter to your people. She'll discuss why history and stories matter, and why they are always-already personal.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 Tutorial

WORKSHOP: In our tutorials, we will discuss what it means to take ownership of a story, or an experience - and what it means to become part of the recovery of a story, an experience or a language. We will discuss this both from the point of view of Anita Heiss (as a writer and historian) and Samantha Disbray (who works in Indigenous language revitalisation). You will need to revisit the Samantha Disbray's lecture segment from week 7 for this class.

PRACTICAL EXERCISE: Storying Exercise

REQUIRED READING: Bunda & Phillips, Heiss, Disbray through Blackboard.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 12 Tutorial WORKSHOP: In our tutorials, we will discuss what it means to take ownership of a story, or an experience - and what it means to become part of the recovery of a story, an experience or a language. We will discuss this both from the point of view of Anita Heiss (as a writer and historian) and Samantha Disbray (who works in Indigenous language revitalisation). You will need to revisit the Samantha Disbray's lecture segment from week 7 for this class. PRACTICAL EXERCISE: Storying Exercise REQUIRED READING: Bunda & Phillips, Heiss, Disbray through Blackboard.

learning_activities.topic:
INDIVIDUAL INTERVIEWS

You will be given a time slot for a 15-minute graduate mock-job interview.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: INDIVIDUAL INTERVIEWS You will be given a time slot for a 15-minute graduate mock-job interview.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13 Lecture: CULTURAL TRAJECTORIES: Relationships & Relationality

METHODS LECTURE SEGMENT: This final lecture of the course, will serve as a summary of the various trajectories explored. We will focus on the centrality of relationships and relationality - in professional life, research and our community as a whole.

SECOND LECTURE SEGMENT: In the second half of the lecture, one of our previous Advanced Humanities students, Jocelyn Flynn, who is now Assistant Curator at the UQ Art Museum will join us to talk about how she has developed her cultural curatorial relationships.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13 Tutorial

WORKSHOP: In our final tutorials, Jocelyn Flynn will join us and lead an exercise where you will be encouraged to explore your relationships and consider what it means to be a good ally, mentor and mentee. We will discuss what we have learnt in the course, and we will will consider avenues for future growth.

PRACTICAL EXERCISE: Final Course Storying Exercise

REQUIRED READING: TBA

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
SHUT-UP-AND-WRITE SESSION

This optional shut-up-and-write session running over Zoom on Monday 2/11 at 9am-11am, will be another opportunity to try out a traditional 'Pomodoro session'. For those of you who are doing honours, this could be an opportunity to start drafting your honours proposals. Alternatively, you can use this time to work on one of your assessments from another course.
